nativeRange.habitat: Cultivated hybrid not known from the wild; the parent species (Anubias barteri and Anubias heterophylla/congensis) are native to shaded streams, river margins, and forest swamps in tropical West and Central Africa, where they grow epiphytically on wood and rocks in submerged or emersed conditions

decorPreferences: rocks and hardscape (e.g. Seiryu, Ohko Dragon Stone)
shaded placement to reduce leaf algae
rhizome must remain above the substrate

goodWith: most African cichlids (tough leaves resist nibbling)

summary: A hardy mid-sized Anubias hybrid (likely Anubias barteri x heterophylla) with broad, pointed leaves. Generally considered very beginner-friendly; rhizome must be tied to hardscape, never buried.
careNotes: Anubias 'Frazeri' is a cultivated hybrid Anubias widely traded in the aquarium hobby. Most sources treat it as a horticultural cross — commonly cited as Anubias barteri x Anubias heterophylla (also called A. congensis) — rather than a botanically described wild species. Some retailers therefore list it as 'Anubias sp. Frazeri' or 'Anubias Frazeri' without committing to species rank. The plant is rumored to have been created by hobbyist Ed Frazer, after whom it is named. Mature plants reach roughly 15–35 cm tall with broad, pointed, lance-to-arrow-shaped leaves that are tougher and more substantial than smaller cultivars like Anubias nana 'Petite'. Its size and form make it best suited to midground placement, although taller specimens can be used as background plants in nano and small community tanks. Care is essentially identical to other Anubias: low to medium lighting is preferred (excess light combined with slow growth invites leaf algae), CO2 injection is optional rather than required (it speeds growth somewhat but is not necessary), and growth is slow regardless of conditions. As with all Anubias, the rhizome — the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge — MUST NOT BE BURIED in substrate. Burial reliably causes the rhizome to rot. Instead, attach the plant to driftwood, rocks, or other hardscape using cotton thread, fishing line, or a small dab of cyanoacrylate (super) glue; the roots will eventually anchor on their own. The plant feeds primarily from the water column, so liquid fertilization is more impactful than substrate fertilization. Temperature range based on cross-source consensus (Modern Aquarium 70–82 °F, Aquatic Arts 72–80 °F, Light Fish 72–82 °F); 22–28 °C represents the comfortable mid-range. pH tolerance is broad in trade descriptions (5.5–9.0 cited by Aquatic Arts; 6.5–7.2 by Light Fish); 6.0–7.8 is a safer practical range. Tank-size note: one source cited a 2.5-gallon minimum, which is impractically small for any realistic display tank; 60 L is used here as a sensible floor matching the related Anubias heterophylla entry.
breedingNotes: Propagated by rhizome division: with a clean sharp blade, cut a healthy rhizome into sections, ensuring each section retains at least 2–3 healthy leaves and some attached roots. Re-attach each section to hardscape — do not bury. New roots typically establish within about two weeks. Like other Anubias, the plant can flower underwater, producing a small spathe-and-spadix inflorescence typical of the Araceae family, but as a hybrid it is not reliably true-to-type from seed and is propagated only vegetatively.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed: midground-to-background placement, low-to-moderate lighting, slow growth, CO2 not required, easy care, broad arrow-shaped leaves, rhizome-must-not-be-buried instruction, temperature 70–82 °F (21–28 °C).

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Used as effective secondary after the designated secondary (Buce Plant) returned HTTP 404. Confirmed: hybrid parentage stated as Anubias barteri x A. heterophylla, family Araceae, temperature 72–80 °F (22–27 °C), pH 5.5–9.0 tolerance, low-to-moderate light, CO2 not strongly responsive, slow growth, midground placement, rhizome-must-not-be-fully-buried, easy difficulty, narrower/more pointed leaves than other Anubias.

additional.name: Light Fish
additional.notes: Third source for triangulation. Confirmed: 'Anubias sp. Frazeri' naming convention, hybrid origin (suggested Anubias congensis x A. barteri, rumored to be a hobbyist cross by Ed Frazer), temperature 72–82 °F (22–28 °C), pH 6.5–7.2, GH 2–15 dGH, KH 3–8 dKH, height 15–18 cm, low light tolerance, CO2 optional, rhizome-must-not-be-buried, easy difficulty, propagation by rhizome cutting with root establishment in about 2 weeks.
